title: practical english usage - Part 309
content:
Unlike comparatives, different can also be modified by very.

I thought you'd be like your sister, but you're quite different.

Quite different means ‘ completely different' (►   564.3).

The new school isn't much  different from the old one.

His ideas are little different from those of his friends.

‘ How's the patient, doctor? ‘ No different.’ I hadn't seen her for years, but she wasn't any different.

by any and no, (a) little and not much.

Different is a little like a comparative: unlike most adjectives, it can be modified any     different, 1  modifiers:              etc 433 different For expressions like the dead (= dead people), ►     188.

After his death his wife went to live in Canada.

Death is a noun meaning ‘ the end of life'.

Note the spelling of the present participle dying (►  348).

So far 50 people have died in the fighting.

She died in a car crash, (     She is dead in ...) not Shakespeare died in 1616. (     Shakespeare-dead-. ..) Died is the past tense and past participle of the verb die.

That idea has been dead for years.

a dead man Mrs McGinty is dead.

Dead is an adjective.

432 dead,      died     and   death I dare you to run across the road with your eyes shut.

frightening things.

Children use the expression I dare you + infinitive to challenge each other to do I dare say it'll rain soon. I daresay you're ready for a drink.

expression meaning ‘ I think probably', 'ɪ suppose'.

And I dare say (sometimes written I daresay) is a rather old-fashioned Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary' •  434   disinterested It's time I did some letter-writing.

I want to do some bird-watching this weekend.

But do can be used with a compound noun that includes verb + object.

(     I'm going to do some watching-TV?) Note that the -ing form in this structure is noun-like, and cannot have an object.

I hate doing the ironing.

During the summer  I'm going to do some walking and a lot of reading.

a determiner (e.g. the, my, some, much)  before the -ing form.

a certain time, or are repeated (for example jobs and hobbies). There is usually We   use do in the informal structure do .. .ing to talk about activities that take 3  do   .. .ing Could you do the ironing first, and then do the windows if you've got time?

Has Ben done his homework?

I did (= studied) French and German at school.

It's time to do the accounts.      I wouldn't like to do your job.

I'm not going to do any work today.  Could you do the shopping for me?

We   use do when we  talk about work and jobs.

Do something! I like doing nothing, (     .. .-makmg-n&lhing) not Then he did a very strange thing, (     Then  made awry strange thing) for example with words like thing, something, nothing, anything, what.

We   use do when we  do not say exactly what activity we  are talking about - 1  do   for   indefinite       activities (advertisement) Learn more about what we do and what we make at megamega.com.

with make.

The  general-purpose verb do has several uses, and can sometimes be confused 435 do:  general-purpose           verb;   do   and   make *523 divorce Disinterested is also often used to mean ’ uninterested’ .

one of the teams.

The referee was not exactly disinterested: his brother-in-law was playing for disagreement or competition.

A  disinterested person has no reason to support one side or another in a 434 disinterested vocabulary' •  437   dress This is the first time I've seen you wearing a dress.

the shoulders to below the hips).

The  countable noun dress means an article of women’ s clothing (it goes from 1  noun 437 dress              ___       _ ►    61 4 down For no doubt meaning ‘ probably’ , ►    534.

I don't doubt (that) there will be more problems.

After negative forms of doubt, we  normally use that or no conjunction.

I doubt we'll have enough money for a new car.

In an informal style, people sometimes use no conjunction.

The directors doubt that new machinery is really necessary.

I doubt if she'll come this evening.

Economists doubt whether interest rates will fall in the near future.

Clauses after the verb doubt can be introduced by whether, if or that.
